DELTAMOUTH — The water came in the night, and by morning the lower half of Deltamouth had vanished beneath a brown, slow-moving tide that carried fence posts, plastic barrels, and the occasional startled cat clinging to a floating door. Three days later, as the river finally began to fall, the town emerged blinking into a changed landscape, its streets slicked with mud and its people counting what the flood had taken and what, against the odds, it had left behind.

The Deltamouth flood was not the largest the river valley has ever seen, but it was among the fastest. A stalled storm dumped nearly three hundred millimetres of rain across the upper watershed in a single day, and the runoff funnelled downstream through a network of channels that had not been dredged in a decade. When the crest reached Deltamouth a little after two in the morning on Sunday, the river rose more than four metres in under six hours, overtopping the earthen levee that had protected the town's riverside district since the 1950s.

Residents described being woken by neighbours pounding on doors, by the wail of the town's single flood siren, and in some cases by the cold shock of water rising around their beds. Many had only minutes to gather what they could and climb to higher ground or to their rooftops, where they waited in the dark for the boats.

"I heard the siren and I thought, not again, it always sounds and it is always nothing," said Marisol Vent, who lives with her two children in a low brick house three streets from the river. "Then I put my foot on the floor and the floor was a river. We went out the window onto the porch roof and we sat there until the sun came up and a man in a rowboat took us off."

The rescue effort drew boats from three neighbouring towns, along with a volunteer flotilla of local fishermen who spent Sunday threading their skiffs between the rooftops and treetops of the drowned district, calling out and listening for answers. By the fire chief's count, more than four hundred people were plucked from roofs, upper windows, and the tops of vehicles in the first twelve hours. Remarkably, the town recorded no deaths, a fact officials attributed to the siren, the speed of the volunteer response, and a good deal of luck.

The scenes along the waterline on Monday were, by turns, heartbreaking and absurd. A grand piano sat upright in the middle of Canal Street, deposited there by the receding water, its lid open and its keys furred with silt. A refrigerator floated in the doorway of the bakery, turning slowly. Someone's wedding photographs, still in their frames, were found scattered across a hedge two streets from the house they had hung in. A goat, no one's goat that anyone could identify, stood placidly on the flat roof of the pharmacy, having apparently ridden the flood to that unlikely perch and declined all attempts at rescue.

For the shopkeepers of Canal Street, the town's low-lying commercial spine, the flood was a catastrophe measured in ruined stock and buckled floors. Water had risen to shoulder height in most of the shops, soaking everything below the waterline and leaving a greasy tidemark on the walls that Monday's survivors were already, grimly, beginning to scrub.

Aldo Reyes, whose family has run the corner grocery on Canal Street for three generations, stood in the wreck of his shop on Monday shovelling spoiled produce into a wheelbarrow. The floodwater had lifted his shelving from the walls and tipped it into the aisles, and a slick of mud coated everything it had touched. "Forty years my father stood behind that counter," he said, gesturing at a counter now on its side in a corner. "I will stand behind it again. But not this week, and not next."

The town's response, in the first hours after the water began to fall, was improvised and communal. The community hall on the hill, the only large building above the flood line, became a shelter, a kitchen, a clinic, and a lost-and-found all at once. Volunteers set up rows of camp beds in the main hall and long tables of donated food in the corridor, and a retired nurse turned the cloakroom into a first-aid station where she treated the cuts, the trench foot, and the shock that come with a flood.

By Monday afternoon the hall was sheltering more than two hundred people, and the smell of soup and wet clothing hung thick in the air. Children who had lost their homes ran between the camp beds playing tag, while their parents stood in clusters, phones pressed to their ears, trying to reach relatives and insurers. A whiteboard by the door filled steadily with messages: families looking for one another, offers of spare rooms in the hillside neighbourhoods, a plea for anyone with a working generator.

The generator, it turned out, was the crux of the town's first great problem. The flood had drowned the substation that fed the lower town, and with it the pumps that might have cleared the standing water from the streets. Until power was restored, the town could only wait for the river to drain the district on its own, a slow business complicated by the very levee that had failed to keep the water out and now, breached in a single place, threatened to keep it in.

Engineers from the provincial utility arrived Monday and set to work on the substation, but they warned that restoring power safely to flooded buildings would take days, not hours. Every drowned outlet, every soaked fuse box, was a potential fire, and the crews moved through the district building by building, cutting power at the meter and tagging each house before anyone could be allowed back inside.

Meanwhile the levee itself became a gathering point. On Monday evening dozens of residents walked out along its muddy crest to look at the breach, a ragged gap perhaps thirty metres wide where the river had simply pushed the old earthwork aside. Through the gap the river still slid, brown and patient, into the streets beyond. Someone had planted a hand-lettered sign at the edge of the breach that read, simply, "REBUILD IT HIGHER."

Among the most harrowing episodes of the flood's first hours was the evacuation of the Rosewater care home, a single-storey building on the eastern edge of the drowned district that housed thirty-eight elderly residents, many of them unable to walk. The water reached the home a little before three in the morning, and the night staff — three young aides and a cook who had stayed late — found themselves waist-deep and rising, wheeling beds toward the only high ground the building offered, the flat roof of an attached storeroom reachable by a short flight of stairs.

The mayor herself was among those who came for them. Roused by a phone call from the home's director, she drove as far as the water would allow, waded the last two streets, and spent the next four hours helping to carry residents up the storeroom stairs and, from there, into the boats that nosed one by one against the roof's edge. "We passed them down hand to hand, wrapped in whatever we had," she said. "Blankets, curtains, a shower curtain in one case. Not one of them let go. Not one of them panicked. They had seen worse things than water in their lives, most of them, and they kept their heads while we lost ours."

The last resident off the roof was a ninety-four-year-old former schoolteacher named Bernard Osei, who had insisted the others go first and who, as the boat carried him away across the black water, was heard to remark that in eighty years in the valley he had been flooded four times and this was only the second worst. He is now, like the others, safe and dry in a converted gymnasium in the hillside town of Upper Fenn, where the care home's residents were taken to wait out the recovery.

The town's school, a sturdy two-storey building that sat just above the worst of the water, came through with only its ground floor flooded, and by Tuesday its upper rooms had been pressed into service as an annex to the overflowing community hall. The gymnasium became a warehouse for donated goods — clothing, nappies, tinned food, bottled water — sorted into towering, teetering stacks by a volunteer army of teenagers who might otherwise have been in the classrooms below. A geography teacher, surveying the piles, observed drily that his students were learning more about supply chains and drainage basins in a week of flood than in a term of lessons.

Insurance, or the lack of it, emerged over the week as the flood's long shadow. Much of the lower town lies in a zone that insurers had quietly reclassified as high-risk after the flood of twelve years ago, and premiums had climbed so steeply since that many households and small businesses had simply let their flood cover lapse. Aldo Reyes, the grocer, was among them. "You gamble," he said. "Every year the bill comes and every year the river stays in its banks and you think, this year I will save the money. And then one year you lose the bet, and you lose everything with it."

Provincial officials promised emergency relief, and a first tranche of grants was announced on Tuesday for households that had lost their homes. But the sums were modest against the scale of the damage, and the application process, residents complained, required documents — deeds, receipts, identity papers — that in many cases were now brown pulp drying on a hedge somewhere downstream. A row of tables appeared in the community hall corridor where volunteers helped flood victims reconstruct, from memory and from whatever scraps survived, the paper trail of their drowned lives.

Whether the levee will be rebuilt higher, and who will pay for it, is the question now hanging over Deltamouth, and it is a question tangled in the same provincial budget fight that has stalled road repairs across the valley. The town's mayor, who spent the flood's first night helping to carry the elderly out of a drowning care home, was blunt about the stakes. "We were told this was a hundred-year flood," she said. "We had one twelve years ago. The arithmetic does not work anymore, and everyone in this valley knows it."

Climate scientists have said for years that the region's rainfall is growing more intense and less predictable, concentrating into fewer, heavier storms of the kind that overwhelmed Deltamouth's defences. The upper watershed, cleared of much of its forest over the past century, sheds that rain faster than it once did, sending it downstream in sharper and higher pulses. The channels that carry the river through the lower valley, narrowed by decades of silt and construction, struggle to contain those pulses even in an ordinary year.

None of this is news to the people of Deltamouth, who have watched the river creep higher up the levee in flood after flood. What has changed, many said this week, is the sense that the old defences and the old assumptions no longer hold, and that the town must either remake itself for a wetter future or slowly surrender its lower half to the water.

For now, though, the work is smaller and more immediate. It is the shovelling of mud, the drying of photographs, the hunt for a missing pet, the slow accounting of a soaked and ruined ground floor. It is the volunteer with the pressure washer working his way down Canal Street, and the line of people passing sandbags hand to hand to shore up the breach against the next high water. It is the free tram of small-town disaster: everyone doing what they can, for as long as they can, for whoever needs it.

On Tuesday the sun came out for the first time in a week, and the flooded district steamed gently as the water dropped and the mud began, at last, to dry. Residents returned to their tagged and darkened houses in ones and twos, stepping over the tidemark on the threshold, to see what could be saved. Furniture and carpet and sodden mattresses piled up along the kerbs, waiting for trucks that had not yet come. From the community hall on the hill the smell of soup still drifted down over the drying town.

Marisol Vent went back to her house on Tuesday afternoon with her two children and a borrowed mop. The water had reached the windowsills and left the rooms brown and stinking, but the walls were sound and the roof, the roof they had sat on through the long dark hours of Sunday morning, was whole. She stood in the doorway for a long moment, taking it in.

"It is a mess," she said at last, wringing the mop into the mud. "But it is still standing, and so are we. You start with the mud. You always start with the mud." Behind her, her son had already found the goat from the pharmacy roof, which had come down at some point during the day and now stood in the ruined front garden, chewing contentedly on the wreckage of a hedge, entirely unbothered, as though floods came and went and a goat's business went on regardless.

Down at the river the water kept falling, an inch an hour, uncovering the lost furniture of a town one soaked and silted street at a time. The engineers worked on the substation. The volunteers worked on the breach. The whiteboard by the hall door kept filling with names, and one by one, slowly, the names were being crossed off and found.
